raw_consumer_statement:
From me American Express collected five insurance premium over-payments totaling {$250.00}. Of these AmEx refunded me only {$100.00}. Hence AmEx owes me another {$150.00} ( {$250.00} - {$100.00} = {$150.00} ). 

According to the attachment from your records and your analysis you, American Express, tell me that - On XX/XX/year>, ( my ) account was billed {$50.00}, which provided ( insurance ) coverage through XX/XX/year>. On XX/XX/year>, American Express received ( my ) payment in the amount of {$100.00}, leaving ( my ) account with a {$0.00} balance due as of the closing date XX/XX/year>. 

You conclude- {$0.00} balance due on XX/XX/year> '' in error. 

Instead, as of the closing date XX/XX/year>, after on XX/XX/year> having just made a double-payment in the amount of {$100.00}, I had a {$50.00} credit coming that you disregard. 

Then you say - On XX/XX/year>, ( my ) account was billed {$50.00}, which provided ( me ) coverage through XX/XX/XXXX, XXXX in error. 

To the contrary, after I had just made a double-payment on XX/XX/year>, it follows that on XX/XX/year> you billed me an extra {$50.00} in error. That is, by double-payment on XX/XX/year>, I had already paid for coverage for two months, from XX/XX/year> through XX/XX/year>. 

Then on XX/XX/year> you received my triple-payment totaling {$150.00} ( {$100.00} + {$50.00} = {$150.00} ). In error you deducted the extra {$50.00} payment that I did not owe, because, as I here repeat, on XX/XX/year> having made a double-payment, I had already paid for coverage through XX/XX/year>. 

Then on XX/XX/year> you received my final double-payment in the amount of {$100.00}. Its all in your attached records. Thus, in error from me you collected five over-payments totaling {$250.00} ( {$150.00} + {$100.00} = {$250.00} ), then refunded me only {$100.00}. So you owe me another {$150.00} ( {$250.00} - {$100.00} = {$150.00} ). Now please refund me the {$150.00} credit balance remaining on my account.
